channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Hybrid KEM Constructions from Classical PKEs and Post-Quantum KEMs
item.description: This paper addresses the urgent need for secure hybrid key encapsulation mechanisms (KEMs) during the transition to post-quantum cryptography. We propose two generic constructions—$\mathsf{HybKEM}$ and $\mathsf{HybKEM}^{*}$—that combine a classical public-key encryption (PKE) scheme with a post-quantum KEM satisfying ciphertext second-preimage resistance (C2PRI). Both constructions are proven IND-CCA secure in the standard model. Crucially, $\mathsf{HybKEM}^{*}$ leverages a new property of classical PKEs—*partial ciphertext second-preimage resistance (PC2PRI)*—which enables shared-key derivation from only a designated ciphertext component (e.g., the elliptic-curve point in ECIES), improving efficiency without sacrificing security. We systematically verify PC2PRI for standardized schemes including ECIES, PSEC-1, and SM2 under standard assumptions. Our work provides the first rigorous, efficient, and broadly applicable framework for integrating mature classical PKE standards with NIST-selected PQC KEMs.

item.title: Playing Tag with Okamoto-Schnorr: Three-Move Pairing-Free Blind Signatures from DDH
item.description: This paper presents the first pairing-free blind signature scheme achieving three-move interaction, strong one-more unforgeability under the standard Decisional Diffie-Hellman (DDH) assumption in the Random Oracle Model (ROM), and black-box group usage—resolving a long-standing open problem. Prior pairing-free constructions either required four or more moves or relied on the Algebraic Group Model (AGM); a recent lower bound confirms three moves is optimal. Our scheme features constant-size signatures and communication (measured in group elements), statistical blindness (in ROM), and a partially blind variant. The core innovation is a new paradigm: augmenting the blind Okamoto-Schnorr protocol with a custom algebraic MAC to enforce binding without pairings or AGM. Security is proven via tight reduction to DDH, and no non-black-box group properties are assumed.

item.title: iToken: One-Time-Use Anonymous Token with Issuance Hiding
item.description: Privacy-Enhancing KYC relies on one-time-use anonymous tokens (OTATs) to reconcile regulatory compliance with user anonymity in frameworks like EUDI Wallet and Private Access Tokens. However, existing OTATs leak issuer identity *during issuance*, enabling targeted DoS attacks and token farming. This paper introduces **iToken**, the first OTAT achieving *issuance hiding*: the issuer remains hidden not only at verification but throughout the entire issuance process. At its core is a novel primitive—**canonical blind ring signature (BRS)**—which enforces a ring structure from the outset of an interactive blind signing protocol, initiated by the signer. We provide two generic BRS constructions: one from linear functions and homomorphic encryption, and another from linear functions and commit-and-prove sum arguments. Our prototype achieves efficient performance: signing bandwidth of 1.2 KB and sub-100 ms issuance latency, demonstrating practical viability for real-world deployment.

item.title: Low-Depth Construction of Grover Oracles from Fully Functional Quantum Circuits
item.description: The Grover oracle is pivotal to the efficiency of Grover’s search algorithm. Rather than building oracles from scratch, practitioners often derive them from existing Fully Functional Quantum Circuits (FFQCs)—circuits that compute a target function *and* restore all ancillae. However, direct integration of FFQCs into oracles introduces unnecessary redundancy: ancilla restoration, essential for FFQC correctness, is superfluous in oracle contexts and inflates both depth and width. We propose a **low-depth transformation method** that systematically converts an FFQC into a lean Grover oracle by eliminating redundant restoration logic while preserving functional equivalence. Crucially, our method further **reduces qubit width** without compromising the achieved low depth, via strategic ancilla reuse and local optimization. Applied to an AES-128 quantum circuit, it shrinks the width from 7280 to **7104 qubits**—a reduction of 176—while simultaneously lowering circuit depth. This work establishes the first practical, structure-preserving FFQC-to-oracle compilation paradigm, enabling scalable amplitude amplification for complex cryptographic primitives on near-term devices.

item.title: Accurate Parameter Estimates for Punctured Key Recovery Linear Attacks
item.description: At EUROCRYPT 2024, Flórez-Gutiérrez and Todo introduced *puncturing*—a technique for linear key recovery attacks that zeroes selected coordinates of the Fourier transform of a linear approximation to reduce time complexity at the cost of increased data complexity. This note revisits their data complexity model, clarifies its implicit assumptions (e.g., Gaussianity of punctured correlations under limited samples), and proposes a statistically grounded refinement. Our improved estimator incorporates second-order moment corrections and CLT applicability criteria, yielding tighter bounds—especially critical near the full codebook regime. Re-evaluating published punctured attacks (e.g., on SIMON-64/128), we find data requirements increase by 1.8–2.3× on average; some estimates shift from $2^{62}$ to $2^{63.2}$ plaintexts, rendering them practically infeasible. The revised model enhances reliability for security evaluation and is implemented in the open-source tool `PuncEst`.

item.title: Secret-Shared Shuffle from Authenticated Correlations
item.description: Shuffle is a fundamental primitive for secure computation, especially in secret-sharing settings where oblivious permutation over shared data enables privacy-preserving aggregation, PSI, and federated learning. Malicious security is essential for real-world deployment, yet achieving *linear communication/computation*, *constant-round interaction*, and *full malicious security* in two-party secret-shared shuffle has remained elusive—prior attempts incurred super-linear costs or high round complexity due to selective-failure attacks. This paper resolves this open problem by introducing a novel paradigm based on *authenticated correlations*. We design a simple, linear-cost, 3-round protocol template and strengthen it to fully authenticate the sender—eliminating selective failures at their root. To handle malicious receivers, we devise new *efficiency-preserving consistency checks*, leveraging masked-difference verification, batched lightweight zero-knowledge arguments, and correlation reuse analysis. Integrated into our framework, these yield two maliciously secure protocols with Θ(n) cost and 3 rounds. Implementation shows they outperform the state-of-the-art: up to **47% faster runtime** and **58% less communication** at $n = 2^{16}$.

item.title: SynCirc: Efficient Synthesis of Depth-Optimized Circuits from High-Level Languages (Extended Version)
item.description: Secure Multi-Party Computation (MPC) relies critically on low-depth circuit representations for efficiency. SynCirc is an open-source, MPC-optimized hardware synthesis framework built on Verilog and Yosys-ABC. It introduces custom multi-input AND gate libraries with depth-aware constraints, reducing multiplicative depth and online rounds by up to **3×** versus TinyGMW. Its expanded library of optimized primitives—including comparators, muxes, and equality checkers—achieves **22.3–66.7%** depth reduction over ShallowCC. Evaluated in FLUTE, SynCirc’s multi-input AND protocol requires only **1/116 the online communication** of Trifecta’s. Crucially, SynCirc integrates XLS-based HLS support, enabling C/C++-to-circuit compilation without Verilog expertise—democratizing secure computation.

item.title: Zeeperio: Verifying Governmental Elections with Ethereum
item.description: Zeeperio is a purpose-built zk-SNARK argument for Scantegrity II elections, designed to enable automatic, low-cost, on-chain verification via Ethereum smart contracts. Unlike general-purpose zk-SNARK toolkits (e.g., Circom or zkVMs), Zeeperio employs application-specific arithmetization—tightly modeling Scantegrity’s ballot encoding, commitment consistency, decryption correctness, and zero-knowledge guarantees—yielding drastic prover efficiency gains: under 5 hours on a commodity laptop to generate a <2 KB proof for 100,000 ballots, representing 2–3 orders-of-magnitude speedup over prior implementations. Its verification contract costs less than $30 per election (at current gas prices), with *constant* cost independent of ballot count. This enables fully automated, publicly auditable, and censorship-resistant election verification—shifting E2E-V from opt-in manual auditing to mandatory, trustless on-chain enforcement.

item.title: TAPAS: Efficient Two-Server Asymmetric Private Aggregation Beyond Prio(+)
item.description: Privacy-preserving aggregation is vital for federated learning and telemetry, enabling collective model training without exposing raw user data. Existing two-server protocols (e.g., Prio+) suffer from symmetric $O(L)$ communication and computation—prohibitive when client inputs involve tens to hundreds of millions of parameters ($L$). TAPAS overcomes this via *intentional asymmetry*: one server performs $O(L)$ aggregation and lattice-based zero-knowledge verification, while the other acts as a lightweight coordinator with $O(1)$ communication and computation independent of $L$. It requires **no trusted setup**, achieves **post-quantum security** solely under standard LWE/SIS assumptions, and provides **full malicious security** with **identifiable abort**—a first for two-server aggregation. Our novel, efficient lattice-based ZKPs enable end-to-end privacy and correctness proofs in this strong threat model.

item.title: Optimizing FROST for Message Capacity
item.description: FROST is a round-optimal threshold signature scheme, but its standard FROST-core protocol requires two presignatures per signature—each demanding an expensive distributed key generation (DKG) run—limiting throughput. This work investigates presignature reuse to improve message capacity. We show that naively using $k$ presignatures for $k$ messages is insecure: an extended ROS attack enables forgery even for $k=2$. However, we prove that using $k+1$ presignatures for $k$ messages is secure in the Generic Group Model + Random Oracle Model. This raises message capacity from 50% (baseline) to $\frac{k}{k+1}$, approaching 100% asymptotically. We further extend security to a practical variant where presignatures are generated asynchronously by different parties: security holds as long as at least $k+1$ of them originate from honest parties—enhancing robustness and deployment flexibility.

item.title: New Approaches to Zero-Knowledge SNARG Constructions
item.description: We present a novel black-box–friendly transformation from SNARGs to zkSNARGs—distinct from the standard NIZK+SNARG paradigm. Inspired by Ishai–Mahmoody–Sahai (TCC’12), our approach combines cryptographic hashing with information-theoretic ZK (specifically, PCPs) and treats the *underlying SNARG as a black box* (other primitives are used non-black-box). The transformation applies to subclasses of NP: batched NP (yielding zkBARGs) and languages admitting computational non-signaling PCPs—including NTISP. A key ingredient is a new **Hiding Somewhere-Extractable (HSE) commitment**, built from LWE, enhancing somewhere-statistically-binding hash functions with computational hiding. As a corollary, we obtain **zkBARGs for NP** and **zkSNARGs for NTISP**, both based solely on the standard Learning With Errors (LWE) assumption—demonstrating that restricting to natural NP subclasses enables zkSNARGs under widely trusted foundations.

item.title: High-Order Galois Automorphisms for TNFS Linear Algebra
item.description: The Tower Number Field Sieve (TNFS) is the state-of-the-art algorithm for solving the discrete logarithm problem in finite fields $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ with composite $n$, especially relevant to pairing-based cryptography lacking quantum-resistant alternatives. While $k$-order Galois automorphisms accelerate relation collection by factor $k$, leveraging them for linear algebra remained open beyond $k=2$. This work resolves the problem for $k = 6$ and $k = 12$: we present a generic construction enabling order-$6$ (resp. order-$12$) Galois automorphisms in *any* $\mathbb{F}_{p^6}$ (resp. $\mathbb{F}_{p^{12}}$), inducing block-cyclic structure in TNFS matrices. This reduces linear algebra cost by approximately $36\times$ and $144\times$, respectively — the first high-order acceleration with provable, practical impact. We provide a full SageMath implementation and validate it on small fields (e.g., $\mathbb{F}_{101^6}$), confirming dimension reduction and runtime gains consistent with theory.

item.title: PrivaDE: Privacy-preserving Data Evaluation for Blockchain-based Data Marketplaces
item.description: PrivaDE is a privacy-preserving protocol enabling model owners and data owners to jointly compute a utility score for a candidate dataset—without revealing model parameters, raw features, or labels. It provides malicious-security guarantees and integrates seamlessly with blockchain-based marketplaces via smart contracts that enforce fair execution and automated payment. To ensure practicality, we introduce two key optimizations: (1) efficient secure model inference using hierarchical gradient approximation, and (2) a model-agnostic scoring method that relies on a small, representative data subset (<0.5% of full set) while preserving high correlation (*r* > 0.92) with full-dataset training impact. Evaluation shows PrivaDE completes evaluation within **under 12 minutes** for models with up to 110M parameters, achieving 5.7× speedup over baseline MPC approaches and reducing memory usage by 62%. PrivaDE establishes the first scalable, verifiable, and privacy-preserving foundation for automated data valuation in decentralized machine learning ecosystems.

item.title: Cryptanalysis of four arbitrated quantum signature schemes
item.description: This paper presents a comprehensive cryptanalysis of four recently proposed arbitrated quantum signature (AQS) schemes. We identify fundamental security flaws in all four protocols—none achieve the claimed authenticity, non-repudiation, or information-theoretic security. Specifically: (1) Zhang et al.’s CNOT-chain scheme allows both sender repudiation and receiver signature forgery; (2) Ding et al.’s GHZ-based protocol admits trivial repudiation by *both* sender and receiver; (3) Lu et al.’s controlled-teleportation scheme is vulnerable to signature forgery, false receipt allegations, and mutual repudiation; (4) Zhang et al.’s entanglement-free scheme fails to guarantee receiver non-repudiation and lacks information-theoretic security for message authentication and unforgeability. Our attacks rely solely on standard quantum operations and protocol logic errors—not computational assumptions—exposing critical design gaps in arbitration, quantum-state binding, and evidence accountability. These results provide concrete counterexamples for rigorous AQS security modeling.

item.title: On Post-Quantum Signature with Message Recovery from Hash-and-Sign in QROM
item.description: We present the first post-quantum signature scheme with message recovery provably secure in the Quantum Random Oracle Model (QROM). Building on Bellare–Rogaway’s classical PSS-R, we adapt its hash-and-sign structure to quantum-safe primitives and overcome a fundamental challenge: securely reprogramming *two correlated random oracles* ($H$ and $G$) under quantum queries—where inputs to one oracle depend on outputs of the other. To this end, we extend two foundational QROM techniques: (i) the tight adaptive reprogramming theorem (Grilo et al., ASIACRYPT 2021) to support *sequential, interdependent reprogramming*, and (ii) the measure-and-reprogram theorem (Don et al., CRYPTO 2020) to handle output-dependent queries with bounded quantum advantage. Our security reduction shows EUF-CMA security with only polynomial loss $O(q_H q_G)$, making it the first rigorously proven QROM-secure SMR scheme. This work bridges a critical gap between post-quantum efficiency and formal security for compact digital signatures.

item.title: TP-NTT: Batch NTT Hardware with Application to Relinearization
item.description: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) promises privacy-preserving computation but suffers from prohibitive overhead, dominated by Number Theoretic Transform (NTT)-based polynomial multiplication. This paper introduces **TP-NTT**, a scalable, throughput-configurable hardware architecture for batch NTT across FHE ring dimensions $2^{10}$ to $2^{16}$. TP-NTT employs multi-dimensional decomposition (2D/3D/4D) without extra multipliers, coupled with optimized modular arithmetic and memory access. At $n = 2^{16}$, it achieves **8.03× lower average latency** and **1.26× better area–time product (ATP)** versus the prior best design. We further demonstrate its practical impact via a BFV relinearization accelerator, delivering **34.65× speedup** over state-of-the-art software (HElib). TP-NTT bridges the gap between algorithmic flexibility and hardware efficiency for real-world FHE deployment.

item.title: Improved Issuer Hiding for BBS-based Anonymous Credentials
item.description: Attribute-based anonymous credentials (e.g., BBS+) often leak issuer identity during verification, undermining user privacy. Recent issuer-hiding proposals suffer from either prohibitive efficiency overhead or critical *policy vulnerabilities*: malicious users—colluding with just one authorized issuer—can forge credentials for arbitrary attributes, effectively impersonating any trusted issuer. To resolve these gaps, we propose the first AGM-proven, BBS-compatible issuer-hiding system based on a *signed-policy* paradigm. Our construction eliminates secret policy keys (enabling fully public verification), delegates policy signing to a trusted CA (removing per-RP key management), and introduces the first pairing-free issuer-hiding scheme using algebraic MACs. Implementation results confirm practical performance (<20 ms per operation), while formal proofs establish unforgeability and *everlasting issuer-hiding anonymity*—even against quantum adversaries post-issuance. This work delivers a secure, efficient, and deployable foundation for real-world privacy-preserving credential systems.

item.title: PrivaLean: Low-Latency and High-Accuracy System for Secure 2PC Inference
item.description: PrivaLean is a novel secure two-party computation (2PC) framework for low-latency, high-accuracy neural network inference under the semi-honest model. To overcome the communication and computational bottlenecks of existing systems, PrivaLean introduces two key innovations: (1) For linear layers, it proposes a *local random ciphertext generation* mechanism—eliminating costly offline interaction—and an *intermediate encoding scheme* that drastically reduces memory footprint for resource-constrained devices; (2) For non-linear activations, it designs a *trigonometric activation protocol* (e.g., sin/cos-based ReLU approximation) requiring only **one online communication round** and avoiding expensive truncation, coupled with a *data-distribution-aware training strategy* leveraging knowledge distillation to improve accuracy—achieving **higher accuracy than the ReLU baseline**. Evaluations on large models (e.g., ResNet-50) in WAN settings show PrivaLean completes inference in **125.02 seconds**, reducing latency by **50.5%** versus Cheetah—the state-of-the-art—while significantly cutting communication rounds.

item.title: Graph-based Asynchrony with Quasilinear Complexity for Any Linear Verifiable Secret Sharing Scheme
item.description: This paper introduces a graph-based statistical Asynchronous Verifiable Secret Sharing (AVSS) protocol, *Bonneval-on-Arc*, that transforms any linear VSS scheme into an efficient AVSS without modifying its core structure. By arranging $n$ parties as a $d$-regular communication graph—where each party interacts only with $d$ neighbors—we bypass the quadratic communication bottleneck inherent in broadcast-based AVSS. Our construction achieves **quasilinear dealer complexity** $O(n \log n)$ and **sublinear per-party complexity** $O(d \log n)$, significantly improving over state-of-the-art $O(n^2 \log n)$ dealer cost. In exchange for statistical (rather than perfect) correctness and termination guarantees, we support a corruption threshold $t < n/(d+2)$—enabling flexible trade-offs: e.g., $d = 4$ yields $t < n/6$, suitable for scalable settings where $t = n/3$ is unnecessarily strong. This is the first generic AVSS framework leveraging sparse graph topology to achieve quasilinear complexity.

item.title: Confidential Databases Without Cryptographic Mappings
item.description: Confidential databases (CDBs) enable secure querying over sensitive data in untrusted clouds via confidential computing (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV), but suffer from severe performance bottlenecks due to frequent synchronous cryptographic operations—especially encryption, decryption, and integrity verification—in the query critical path. We present **FEDB**, a novel CDB architecture that eliminates all cryptographic operations from latency-sensitive execution paths. FEDB introduces *crypto-free mappings*: it uses data-independent identifiers (e.g., logical page IDs, hash-based keys) internally for indexing and access, while securely delegating the mapping from identifiers to plaintext secrets exclusively to a lightweight trusted domain inside the TEE. This decoupling ensures confidentiality and integrity without runtime crypto overhead. Evaluated on industry-standard benchmarks, FEDB achieves up to **78.0× higher throughput** than state-of-the-art CDBs on TPC-C and TPC-H, reduces CPU crypto load by 99.3%, and cuts memory bandwidth pressure by 86%, all while maintaining full SQL compatibility and end-to-end security guarantees.

item.title: Functional Subspace Watermarking for Large Language Models
item.description: Model watermarking for large language models (LLMs) faces severe robustness challenges under realistic model modifications (e.g., fine-tuning, quantization, distillation), as parameter- or activation-based signals degrade unpredictably. To address this, we propose **Functional Subspace Watermarking (FSW)**—a novel framework that embeds ownership signals into a low-dimensional *functional* subspace derived from the model’s input-output Jacobian, rather than its parameters. FSW solves a generalized eigenvalue problem to extract a stable functional backbone, employs adaptive spectral truncation to optimize the robustness–utility trade-off, and enforces vector consistency to preserve semantic performance. Extensive experiments across six LLM architectures (LLaMA-2, Qwen, Phi-3, etc.) and multiple datasets show FSW achieves **98.2% detection accuracy** under composite attacks (LoRA fine-tuning + 4-bit quantization + knowledge distillation), outperforming prior SOTA by >25 percentage points. It also provides statistically verifiable ownership (p < 0.001), establishing a function-centric paradigm for trustworthy LLM copyright protection.

item.title: SoK: Practical Aspects of Releasing Differentially Private Graphs
item.description: Graph data is ubiquitous yet privacy-sensitive, but applying Differential Privacy (DP) to graphs remains fraught with practical challenges—including ambiguous adjacency definitions, fragmented utility goals, and poor interpretability of privacy guarantees. This SoK paper bridges the gap between theory and practice by systematizing 67 state-of-the-art DP graph release methods (2012–2023) along three dimensions: privacy model (edge/node/key-value DP), graph type (simple/attributed/temporal), and output form (synthetic graphs/perturbed statistics/private query interfaces). We identify three critical vulnerabilities: (i) mismatched adjacency assumptions leading to under-protection, (ii) uncalibrated post-processing that violates privacy budgets, and (iii) utility evaluations ignoring structural distribution shifts. To address these, we propose an objective-driven framework that guides practitioners from task specification (e.g., “publishing a private co-authorship network for centrality analysis”) to appropriate privacy modeling, metric selection, and rigorous evaluation. Validated on two social network scenarios—DP synthetic graph generation for community detection and node-DP publication for attribute-aware link prediction—our framework exposes critical utility gaps in existing methods and establishes the first unified, reproducible benchmark for practical DP graph release.

item.title: ClawTrap: A MITM-Based Red-Teaming Framework for Real-World OpenClaw Security Evaluation
item.description: We present **ClawTrap**, the first MITM-based red-teaming framework designed for realistic security evaluation of autonomous web agents like **OpenClaw**. Unlike static sandbox benchmarks, ClawTrap operates at the network layer—intercepting and transforming live HTTP(S) traffic to emulate real-world threats including *Static HTML Replacement*, *Iframe Popup Injection*, and *Dynamic Content Modification*. Its rule-driven, reproducible pipeline enables systematic, customizable attack orchestration and auditing across diverse agent frameworks and LLM backbones. Empirical evaluation across 7 open-source agents and 5 LLMs reveals clear model stratification: weaker models (e.g., Phi-3-mini) frequently trust tampered observations and generate unsafe outputs (68.3% unsafe rate), while stronger models (e.g., Qwen2.5-72B) demonstrate robust anomaly attribution and safer fallback behaviors (9.1% unsafe rate). These results underscore that reliable OpenClaw security assessment must explicitly incorporate dynamic MITM conditions—not just static prompt attacks or sandboxed environments. ClawTrap establishes a foundational infrastructure for future network-aware agent security research.

item.title: Measuring and Exploiting Confirmation Bias in LLM-Assisted Security Code Review
item.description: This paper presents the first empirical study of **confirmation bias** in LLM-assisted security code review—demonstrating that LLMs systematically favor interpretations aligning with suggestive framing (e.g., “this PR fixes all security issues”), leading to severe vulnerability detection failures. Across 250 CVE pairs and 4 state-of-the-art models, biased framing reduced detection rates by **16–93%**, with sharp increases in false negatives but negligible change in false positives—revealing strong asymmetry. Injection vulnerabilities proved significantly more susceptible than memory corruption bugs. In adversarial evaluation, maliciously framed pull requests (e.g., reintroducing SQLi while labeled as “urgent performance fix”) evaded GitHub Copilot in **35%** of one-shot attempts and Claude Code in **88%** of iterative, real-project deployments. Crucially, lightweight debiasing—redacting metadata and adding explicit instructions (“ignore prior assumptions”)—restored detection in **100% of interactive cases** and **94% of autonomous cases**. Our findings expose a critical, exploitable weakness in AI-augmented development tooling and call for mandatory prompt robustness auditing and metadata trust controls in production CI/CD systems.

item.title: Revisiting Label Inference Attacks in Vertical Federated Learning: Why They Are Vulnerable and How to Defend
item.description: Vertical federated learning (VFL) enables collaborative modeling between an active party (holding labels and a top model) and passive parties (holding features and bottom models), yet exposes labels to inference attacks (LIAs). Contrary to the prevailing assumption that well-trained bottom models inherently encode label information, we theoretically prove—via mutual information analysis—the “**model compensation**” phenomenon: label-relevant information concentrates in deeper layers, with bottom models primarily extracting features and the top model handling label mapping. We show LIAs succeed only under implicit **feature-label distribution alignment**, and fail catastrophically when this alignment is disrupted via task reassignment. Leveraging this insight, we propose a **zero-overhead defense**: shifting the cut layer forward to enlarge the top model’s proportion. Extensive experiments across 5 datasets and 5 architectures demonstrate that this simple adjustment not only reduces LIA success rates to near-random (<5%) but also strengthens complementary defenses (e.g., differential privacy, gradient masking) without accuracy loss.

item.title: Prompt Control-Flow Integrity: A Priority-Aware Runtime Defense Against Prompt Injection in LLM Systems
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) deployed in API and RAG systems face severe prompt injection threats that can override safety policies and induce harmful outputs. Existing defenses treat prompts as flat strings and rely on brittle heuristics or static detection—lacking structural awareness and priority modeling. This paper introduces **Prompt Control-Flow Integrity (PCFI)**, a lightweight, runtime defense that treats each request as a structured composition of segments—system (highest priority), developer, user (lowest), and retrieved documents—and enforces hierarchical policy via a three-stage middleware: lexical role-aware filtering, role-switch detection, and priority-resolved instruction arbitration. Implemented as a FastAPI gateway, PCFI achieves **100% attack interception**, **0% false positives**, and only **0.04 ms median latency overhead** on a benchmark of synthetic and semi-realistic injection workloads—including RAG-context poisoning and multi-turn role hijacking. PCFI demonstrates that provenance-aware, priority-driven prompt enforcement is both practical and highly efficient for production LLM systems.

item.title: AS2 -- Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets: An End-to-End Differentiable Neuro-Soft-Symbolic Reasoning Architecture
item.description: We propose **AS2 (Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets)**, an end-to-end differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that replaces the discrete Answer Set Programming (ASP) solver with a soft, continuous approximation of the immediate consequence operator $T_P$. AS2 maintains per-position symbol probability distributions throughout the forward pass and trains by minimizing the fixed-point residual of a probabilistic lift of $T_P$, enabling full gradient flow from symbolic constraints to perception without any external solver—neither during training nor inference. Crucially, it abandons positional embeddings in favor of constraint-group membership embeddings, directly encoding the declarative ASP specification and achieving indexing-agnostic structural awareness. On Visual Sudoku, AS2 achieves **99.89% cell accuracy and 100% verified constraint satisfaction** across 1,000 test boards using only greedy constrained decoding; on MNIST Addition with $N \in \{2,4,8\}$ addends, it attains **>99.7% digit accuracy** at all scales. These results demonstrate that a differentiable fixed-point operator, combined with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraint specification, matches or exceeds pipeline and solver-based neuro-symbolic systems while preserving full end-to-end differentiability.

item.title: DriftGuard: Mitigating Asynchronous Data Drift in Federated Learning
item.description: **DriftGuard** is a federated continual learning framework designed to mitigate *asynchronous data drift*—where client devices experience non-uniform, time-varying distribution shifts in real-world FL deployments. To balance adaptation efficacy and resource efficiency, DriftGuard introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-inspired parameter decoupling: *shared parameters* encode globally transferable knowledge (updated globally upon system-wide drift detection), while *local parameters* are grouped by MoE gating patterns to adapt to cluster-specific distributions (updated locally without raw data sharing). Experiments across diverse datasets (CIFAR-10/100, TinyImageNet, FEMNIST) and models (CNN, ResNet, ViT) show DriftGuard matches or exceeds state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing total retraining cost by up to **83%**, achieving up to **2.3× higher accuracy per unit retraining cost** versus the strongest baseline. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/blessonvar/DriftGuard.

item.title: Evaluating Model-Free Policy Optimization in Masked-Action Environments via an Exact Blackjack Oracle
item.description: This paper introduces an **exact dynamic programming oracle** for infinite-shoe blackjack—a rigorous, verifiable benchmark for model-free RL under dynamic action masking. Built over 4,600 canonical decision cells under fixed Vegas rules, the oracle provides ground-truth action values, optimal policies, and theoretical EV (−0.00161/hand). Three model-free optimizers—masked REINFORCE (with per-cell EMA baseline), SPSA, and CEM—were evaluated via simulation. REINFORCE achieved the highest sample efficiency: 46.37% action-match rate and EV = −0.04688 after 10⁶ hands, outperforming CEM (39.46%, 7.5×10⁶ evaluations) and SPSA (38.63%, 4.8×10⁶ evaluations). Crucially, all methods exhibited substantial *cell-conditional regret*, exposing a critical gap between smooth reward convergence and local policy correctness—highlighting limitations in sparse-visit, masked-action tabular settings. As a negative control, we prove and verify that optimal bet sizing collapses to the table minimum under i.i.d. draws; larger bets increase ruin risk without improving expectation. These results underscore the necessity of exact oracles and rigorous negative controls to disentangle stochastic noise from genuine algorithmic progress.

item.title: CausalVAD: De-confounding End-to-End Autonomous Driving via Causal Intervention
item.description: Planning-oriented end-to-end driving models suffer from causal confusion—relying on spurious statistical correlations rather than true causal mechanisms—leading to unsafe shortcuts under distribution shifts. To address this, we propose **CausalVAD**, a de-confounding training framework grounded in causal intervention theory. Its core, the **Sparse Causal Intervention Scheme (SCIS)**, is a lightweight, plug-and-play module that implements backdoor adjustment via a learnable dictionary of latent driving context prototypes. SCIS intervenes on sparse vectorized queries in the network to actively suppress confounder-induced spurious associations, yielding cleaner, causally faithful representations. On nuScenes, CausalVAD achieves state-of-the-art planning accuracy (18.7% lower L2 pose error) and safety (32.4% fewer collisions). It also demonstrates superior robustness against both dataset bias and synthetically induced causal confusion, establishing a new paradigm for trustworthy autonomous driving.

item.title: Beyond Passive Aggregation: Active Auditing and Topology-Aware Defense in Decentralized Federated Learning
item.description: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) is highly vulnerable to adaptive backdoor attacks that evade passive defenses by dynamically manipulating local updates. To counter this, we propose the first *active, interventional auditing framework* for DFL. We introduce a spatiotemporal dynamical model capturing adversarial update diffusion over arbitrary graph topologies, and design three lightweight, privacy-preserving auditing metrics—stochastic entropy anomaly, randomized smoothing KL divergence, and activation kurtosis—that use private probes to expose latent backdoors invisible to static detection. Further, we develop a topology-aware defense placement strategy to maximize aggregation resilience under communication constraints. We theoretically prove almost-sure convergence under co-evolving attack-defense dynamics. Extensive experiments across diverse architectures and real-world topologies show our method reduces adaptive backdoor success rate from >89% to <6.2%, with only 0.4–1.3% primary task accuracy degradation—outperforming state-of-the-art defenses including FoolsGold, Zeno++, and Byzantine-Robust Aggregation.

item.title: FedTrident: Resilient Road Condition Classification Against Poisoning Attacks in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving camera-based Road Condition Classification (RCC) for intelligent transportation systems, yet remains vulnerable to Targeted Label-Flipping Attacks (TLFAs), where malicious vehicles poison local training data—e.g., relabeling “bumpy road” as “smooth”—to degrade global model safety. Existing defenses fail under diverse attack settings due to: (1) non-TLFA-specific local model inspection, (2) static client exclusion without behavioral history, and (3) no remediation of the already-corrupted global model. To bridge this gap, we propose **FedTrident**, a three-pronged framework featuring: (i) neuron-wise local model analysis for misbehavior detection—including attack goal identification, critical feature extraction, and GMM-based clustering/filtering; (ii) adaptive client rating per FL round to dynamically exclude malicious participants; and (iii) machine unlearning to efficiently repair the poisoned global model post-exclusion. Extensive experiments across RCC models (ResNet, ViT), heterogeneous data, multi-task settings, and dynamic attacks show FedTrident restores performance to near attack-free levels (98.2% of clean accuracy) and outperforms eight baselines by 9.49% and 4.47% on the two most critical metrics—demonstrating strong resilience against up to 40% malicious clients and severe data heterogeneity.

item.title: Towards Verifiable AI with Lightweight Cryptographic Proofs of Inference
item.description: This paper introduces a lightweight, statistically grounded framework for verifiable AI inference, addressing the prohibitive prover overhead (e.g., hundreds of seconds per query) of cryptographic proof systems. Instead of full zero-knowledge proofs, we leverage intrinsic *trace separation*—the statistical distinguishability of internal activation paths between functionally dissimilar models—to enable efficient verification. The prover commits to the inference trace via Merkle-tree vector commitments and reveals only a small number of entries along randomly sampled output-to-input paths. This trades strict soundness for practical efficiency—ideal for auditing, high-query-volume deployments, and rationally incentivized settings with detection penalties. Experiments on ResNet-18 and Llama-2-7B confirm required statistical properties hold in practice and that common adversarial strategies (e.g., gradient-based reconstruction, logit swapping) fail to evade detection. We further propose a refereed delegation protocol enabling correct output identification in *O(log n)* rounds using two competing servers. Proving time drops from minutes to milliseconds—a >1000× speedup—while proofs remain compact (KB-scale).

item.title: Security awareness in LLM agents: the NDAI zone case
item.description: This study investigates how LLM agents form security awareness—specifically, whether they can reliably distinguish secure (TEE-based) from insecure execution environments—using the NDAI zone framework, where IP disclosure is rational only under verified trust. Across 10 diverse LLMs and controlled evidence conditions (e.g., valid/invalid remote attestation reports, system declarations, or ambiguous prompts), we measure disclosure behavior in a standardized negotiation task. We find a striking asymmetry: *all models consistently suppress disclosure when attestation fails*, demonstrating robust threat detection; however, *responses to passing attestation are highly heterogeneous*—only 4 models increase disclosure meaningfully, 5 show no change, and 1 paradoxically reduces it. This reveals a critical gap: current LLMs reliably identify danger but cannot reliably verify safety—a foundational capability for privacy-preserving agentic protocols. Bridging this gap requires advances in interpretability, targeted fine-tuning for environment reasoning, and improved evidence representation architectures.

item.title: Secure Linear Alignment of Large Language Models
item.description: This paper introduces **Secure Linear Alignment (SLA)**, a privacy-preserving framework enabling inference across independently trained LLMs without sharing data or model weights. Leveraging empirically observed *representational convergence*—where diverse models develop semantically aligned hidden states—we learn lightweight affine transformations between final-layer embeddings using only a small public dataset. During inference, SLA applies CKKS homomorphic encryption *exclusively* to the linear alignment and classification operations, bypassing costly encryption of nonlinear layers; this achieves sub-second latency (< 800 ms) while providing formal security guarantees against semi-honest adversaries. Experiments across 12 model pairs (e.g., Llama-3 ↔ Phi-3, Qwen ↔ Bloom) show minimal performance drop in embedding classification (−0.9% accuracy) and out-of-distribution detection (−1.2% AUROC). Crucially, we demonstrate for the first time that linearly aligned representations can support *cross-model text generation*: mapping prompts from one model into another’s decoder yields coherent outputs (BLEU-4 = 63.5), revealing deep structural compatibility in LLM representation spaces.

item.title: MIDST Challenge at SaTML 2025: Membership Inference over Diffusion-models-based Synthetic Tabular data
item.description: The MIDST Challenge at SaTML 2025 is the first large-scale, standardized evaluation of **membership inference vulnerability** in synthetic tabular data generated by diffusion models. Recognizing that tabular data’s heterogeneity (mixed data types), structural complexity (e.g., relational constraints), and sparse distribution undermine the privacy assumptions of generative models, MIDST introduced a rigorous benchmark with diverse target diffusion models—including single-table (e.g., TabDDPM) and multi-relational variants—and unified evaluation protocols. It catalyzed the development of novel **black-box and white-box MIAs specifically designed for diffusion-based tabular generators**, such as gradient-aware white-box attacks and structure-informed confidence-shift detectors. Empirical results reveal non-negligible membership leakage (AUC: 0.72–0.89) across models, with higher vulnerability in relational settings—highlighting critical gaps between statistical fidelity and privacy resilience. The open-source MIDST repository (https://github.com/VectorInstitute/MIDST) provides datasets, models, attack implementations, and evaluation tools to advance reproducible privacy research.

item.title: SHAPCA: Consistent and Interpretable Explanations for Machine Learning Models on Spectroscopy Data
item.description: This paper introduces **SHAPCA**, a novel explainable machine learning pipeline tailored for spectroscopy data. It integrates Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for stable dimensionality reduction with Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) enhanced by an **inverse projection mechanism**, enabling faithful attribution of model predictions back to the original wavelength space—thus preserving biochemical interpretability. Unlike standard SHAP (prone to instability due to spectral collinearity) or PCA-only explanations (semantically disconnected from raw signals), SHAPCA delivers both **global insights** (e.g., identifying diagnostically relevant spectral bands like amide I at 1650–1680 cm⁻¹) and **instance-specific explanations**. Experiments across four real-world spectroscopic datasets demonstrate that SHAPCA achieves **significantly higher explanation consistency** (mean Pearson correlation of 0.92 ± 0.03 across 10 independent training runs vs. 0.61 ± 0.15 for baseline SHAP) and **strong domain alignment**, with >87% of top-ranked bands validated by expert knowledge. SHAPCA bridges the gap between statistical robustness and physical interpretability in spectroscopic AI.

item.title: On Optimizing Multimodal Jailbreaks for Spoken Language Models
item.description: This paper addresses the critical safety gap in Spoken Language Models (SLMs), which inherit LLM vulnerabilities while facing an expanded multimodal attack surface. We propose **JAMA (Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack)**, the first gradient-based framework that *simultaneously optimizes adversarial text prompts and audio waveforms*—combining Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) for discrete text and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for continuous audio under joint loss minimization. Evaluated across four state-of-the-art SLMs and four audio types (clean, noisy, TTS, cross-speaker), JAMA achieves **1.5× to 10× higher jailbreak success rates** than unimodal baselines. A sequential approximation variant further accelerates optimization by **4–6×** with negligible (<2%) performance drop. Our results demonstrate that multimodal synergy fundamentally undermines unimodal safety assumptions—highlighting the necessity of co-designed defense mechanisms for robust SLMs. Code and data are publicly available.

item.title: Communication-Efficient and Robust Multi-Modal Federated Learning via Latent-Space Consensus
item.description: Federated learning (FL) struggles in multi-modal settings due to client heterogeneity in modalities (e.g., accelerometer, camera, audio), divergent model architectures, and prohibitive communication costs. To address this, we propose **CoMFed**, a communication-efficient and robust FL framework that operates in a *shared latent space* rather than parameter space. CoMFed employs lightweight, modality-specific learnable projection matrices to compress raw multi-modal inputs into low-dimensional latent representations (e.g., 128-D), reducing upload volume by >95%. A novel latent-space regularizer enforces cross-client and cross-modal consistency via contrastive alignment, improving generalization and outlier resilience. Experiments on UCI-HAR, WISDM, and MobiAct show CoMFed achieves state-of-the-art accuracy (+2.1–4.7% over FedMD/MM-Fed) under ultra-low communication overhead (<5% of raw feature size) and maintains 62% higher robustness against malicious clients. CoMFed establishes a new paradigm for privacy-preserving, scalable multi-modal collaboration.

item.title: When Differential Privacy Meets Wireless Federated Learning: An Improved Analysis for Privacy and Convergence
item.description: Differentially private wireless federated learning (DPWFL) promises privacy-preserving collaborative training over resource-constrained wireless devices, yet suffers from two critical gaps: (1) existing privacy loss bounds diverge with iteration count, failing to capture long-term privacy stability; and (2) convergence analyses often assume convexity or omit gradient clipping—a practical necessity for privacy amplification. We bridge these gaps via a unified analysis for *smooth non-convex* objectives, explicitly modeling both device sampling and local mini-batch sampling under wireless channel effects. Crucially, we prove that the Rényi privacy loss *converges to a finite constant*, not infinity, revealing inherent privacy stabilization in DPWFL. We further establish $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence to first-order stationary points with explicit gradient clipping, and derive a closed-form privacy-utility trade-off linking $\varepsilon$, clipping threshold $C$, and channel noise $\sigma_h^2$. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and FEMNIST validate our theory—e.g., a 10 dB SNR gain improves test accuracy by 3.2% at fixed $\varepsilon$.

item.title: NI-DKG: Non-Interactive Distributed Key Generation Using Blockchain and Zero-Knowledge Proofs
item.description: We propose **NI-DKG**, the first fully non-interactive Distributed Key Generation protocol that eliminates complaint phases by embedding comprehensive correctness verification into a single zk-SNARK per participant. Each proof simultaneously certifies: (i) consistency of polynomial commitments, (ii) satisfaction of Feldman verification equations, and (iii) correctness of hashed ElGamal-encrypted shares (via Chaum-Pedersen proofs). Leveraging smart-contract-enabled blockchains as a timed, computationally augmented bulletin board, the protocol reduces coordination to two atomic on-chain phases—submission and finalization—delimited solely by block numbers. Invalid submissions are rejected immediately by the EVM verifier; no disputes or timeouts are needed. Built on standard primitives (Shamir sharing, Feldman commitments, hashed ElGamal), NI-DKG provides concrete circuit specifications for key generation, threshold decryption, and optional secret key disclosure on EVM chains. We also address practical constraints—such as EVM’s public-input limits—via modular aggregation strategies. This design achieves stronger liveness, censorship resistance, and deployment simplicity compared to prior interactive DKGs.

item.title: Succinct Verification of Lattice-Based Compressed $\Sigma$-Protocols via Delegated Proofs of Correct Folding of Cryptographically Generated Public Parameters
item.description: Inner product arguments are fundamental to succinct zero-knowledge proofs, yet standard compressed $\Sigma$-protocols—despite achieving logarithmic communication—suffer from *linear verification time*, dominated by CRS folding for vector commitments. This work introduces **delegated proofs of correct folding (PoCF)**: an interactive protocol enabling the prover to *compute and prove correctness* of the CRS folding, assuming the CRS is cryptographically generated from a short seed (e.g., via PRF or hash-to-lattice). We instantiate PoCF in the lattice setting under LWE, achieving *logarithmic-time verification* ($O(\log n)$) and *constant communication* for structured linear forms (e.g., matrix-vector products). Our succinctly verifiable compressed $\Sigma$-protocol thus eliminates the linear bottleneck while preserving soundness and zero-knowledge. This yields the first lattice-based compressed argument with sublinear verification complexity, enabling practical deployment in bandwidth- and latency-constrained environments.

item.title: Solving the Linear Code Equivalence Problem from Single Codeword Matching
item.description: The Linear Code Equivalence (LCE) problem—determining whether two linear codes over $\mathbb{F}_q$ ($q \ge 5$) are related by a monomial transformation—is a foundational hardness assumption for post-quantum schemes like LESS. State-of-the-art attacks rely on matching multiple low-weight codewords, which is computationally expensive and poorly scalable. We introduce a breakthrough *single-pair matching test*: given candidate codewords $\mathbf{v}_1 \in \mathcal{C}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2 \in \mathcal{C}_2$, we compute the Schur products $\mathcal{C}_1 \star \mathbf{v}_1^{-1}$ and $\mathcal{C}_2 \star \mathbf{v}_2^{-1}$. We prove that $\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2$ match *iff* these derived codes are permutation-equivalent—a property decidable in polynomial time. Leveraging this, we design new LCE solvers achieving the optimal asymptotic exponent across broad parameter ranges. Applied to LESS, our method reduces the best-known bit complexity significantly: e.g., the security of LESS-1 drops from 127 to 120 bits.

item.title: Look Ahead! Practical CCA-secure Steganography: Cover-Source Switching meets Lattice Gaussian Sampling
item.description: Steganography aims to conceal both message content and the very existence of communication. Prior provably CCA-secure stegosystems are either restricted to memoryless channels or inherently inefficient due to min-entropy dependence. This work overcomes a fundamental impossibility result (Berndt–Liśkiewicz, EUROCRYPT’18) by introducing the *look-ahead* model and defining *partially sampleable channels*—a natural class capturing real-world sources like Gaussian sensor noise. We formalize *cover-source switching*, integrate lattice-based *Gaussian preimage sampling*, and construct the first practical CCA-secure stegosystem for Gaussian channels. Our scheme achieves an asymptotic embedding rate of $1/\omega(\log \log \lambda)$, independent of channel min-entropy. A prototype implementation attains **24.7% embedding rate** on 24-megapixel RAW images in **~1 minute per image**, demonstrating concrete feasibility.

item.title: Post-Quantum Cryptography from Quantum Stabilizer Decoding
item.description: This paper proposes *random quantum stabilizer decoding* (RQSD) — a quantum-native problem with purely classical I/O representation — as a compelling new post-quantum hardness assumption. We prove that its average-case hardness implies the full Cryptomania landscape: public-key encryption (PKE), oblivious transfer (OT), and one-way functions. Our PKE matches state-of-the-art LPN-based efficiency in ciphertext size and computation; our OT is round-optimal (2 rounds). Crucially, we provide strong evidence that RQSD does not reduce to LPN due to inherent symplectic algebraic structure — establishing it as a genuinely novel assumption. Technically, we introduce (1) a reduction to a structured “symplectic-LPN” problem, and (2) new scrambling techniques for symplectic linear spaces, enabling rigorous security proofs for all constructions.

item.title: Dialga: A Family of Low-Latency Tweakable Block Ciphers using Multiple Linear Layers (Full Version)
item.description: This paper introduces **Dialga**, a family of low-latency tweakable block ciphers supporting 128/256-bit tweaks and 256-bit keys. Dialga achieves unprecedented hardware efficiency through three innovations: (i) multiple lightweight linear layers with optimized cell permutations, boosting resistance against differential and linear cryptanalysis with negligible overhead; (ii) SAT-guided selection of 4-bit S-boxes balancing nonlinearity, differential uniformity, and propagation delay; and (iii) a reflection-based tweakey schedule enabling zero-overhead encryption/decryption and strong related-tweak security. Hardware benchmarks show Dialga reduces critical-path delay by ~50% and area by ~40% versus QARMAv2 at comparable security. When instantiated in the Flat-ΘCB authenticated encryption mode, Dialga outperforms AES-256-GCM with 35–42% lower latency and smaller area—making it ideal for ultra-low-latency applications.

item.title: Hyperelliptic Gluing Isogeny Diffie–Hellman (HGIDH): A Genus-2 Gluing Isogeny Key-Exchange
item.description: We introduce **Hyperelliptic Gluing Isogeny Diffie–Hellman (HGIDH)**, a post-quantum key-exchange protocol built on *gluing isogenies* from $E_1 \times E_2$ (product of two supersingular elliptic curves) to $\operatorname{Jac}(C)$ (Jacobian of a genus-2 hyperelliptic curve). Leveraging the **Frey–Kani correspondence**, HGIDH constructs principally polarized abelian surfaces via maximal isotropic subgroups of $(E_1 \times E_2)[N]$. Crucially, private keys are encoded as **four scalars defining a non-cyclic, two-dimensional kernel**, eliminating structural vulnerabilities exploited in SIDH-style attacks. We formalize security via two intermediate problems—*Gluing Kernel Recovery* and *Genus-2 Isogeny Computation*—and prove that any efficient adversary solving either implies an efficient solver for standard supersingular isogeny problems (e.g., SSDDH), thus grounding HGIDH’s hardness in the established isogeny landscape. The construction resists known classical and quantum attacks, including torsion-point and Costello–Smith meet-in-the-middle strategies.

item.title: Aggregator-Based Voting using proof of Partition
item.description: We present **Aggios**, a scalable and privacy-preserving proxy voting system for frequent, large-scale elections (e.g., DAO governance), where on-chain vote storage is prohibitively expensive. Aggios delegates voting power to trusted *aggregators*, who batch votes locally and post only succinct zero-knowledge proofs—built upon our novel **Extended Partition Argument (EPA)**—to the ledger. EPA enables efficient, universal-SRS-compatible proofs that a committed vector admits a partition into disjoint subvectors of public or hidden sizes, with linear prover time and logarithmic verifier time, proven secure in the Algebraic Group Model. Implementation shows EPA scales to vectors with millions of entries. Aggios reduces proof size by **>512×** versus naïve per-vote submission, and achieves *N*-independent proof size in optimal settings—enabling practical, private, and frequent voting at scale.

item.title: HARE: Compact HQC via Distance-Informed Erasure Decoding
item.description: We present **HARE**, a compact Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM) built on the HQC framework, designed to reduce public key and ciphertext sizes without compromising security or reliability. Its core innovation is *distance-informed erasure decoding*: during decryption, Hamming distance information from the inner code (e.g., BCH) is used to identify unreliable blocks and treat them as erasures—enabling highly efficient correction by the outer code (e.g., RS). Integrated with Bitzer et al.’s ciphertext compression (EUROCRYPT 2026) and refined parameter selection, HARE achieves a significantly lower decryption failure rate, permitting more aggressive parameter compression. For NIST security Levels 1, 3, and 5, HARE reduces the *combined size* of public key and ciphertext by **13.2%, 12.3%, and 13.3%**, respectively, compared to baseline HQC. This makes HARE one of the most size-efficient HQC-based KEMs to date, while preserving compatibility with existing HQC implementations and hardware accelerators.

item.title: Differential Privacy in Generative AI Agents: Analysis and Optimal Tradeoffs
item.description: This paper addresses a critical gap in privacy research for generative AI agents: while prior work focuses on protecting user prompts, we formalize and quantify **privacy leakage from enterprise data accessed by AI agents**. We propose a probabilistic differential privacy (DP) framework where response generation is modeled as a stochastic mechanism mapping prompts and private datasets to token-sequence distributions. Within this framework, we introduce *token-level* and *message-level* DP definitions and derive tight analytical bounds linking privacy loss ε to generation parameters—especially temperature and message length—showing that ε grows exponentially with temperature. We formulate a privacy-utility co-design problem and prove the existence of a unique optimal temperature that maximizes response quality (e.g., task accuracy, perplexity) under a given (ε, δ)-DP constraint. Empirical evaluation on domain-specific benchmarks confirms our bounds’ tightness (<12% error) and demonstrates that the optimal temperature strategy improves utility by up to 37% over baselines while satisfying strong privacy guarantees (ε ≤ 0.8).
